## Summary
The Hubble tension refers to the persistent 4–6σ discrepancy between the Hubble constant measured from the local distance ladder (H₀ ≈ 73 km/s/Mpc) and the value inferred from the early universe via CMB observations assuming ΛCDM (H₀ ≈ 67.4 km/s/Mpc). No consensus explanation exists.

## Evidence
The SH0ES team measures H₀ = 73.04 ± 1.04 km/s/Mpc using Cepheid-calibrated Type Ia supernovae. Planck CMB data yield H₀ = 67.36 ± 0.54 km/s/Mpc. Independent methods (TRGB, surface brightness fluctuations, strong lensing time delays) give intermediate values. JWST Cepheid observations have largely confirmed HST distance ladder calibrations.

## Significance
The Hubble tension is arguably the most pressing open problem in cosmology. If not resolved by systematic errors, it points to new physics — possibly early dark energy, extra relativistic species, modified gravity, or a fundamental revision of the standard cosmological model.
